sentences.text: Well, as-- to them, I spoke perfectly Slovakian.
So they had no-- no way of detecting that I'm not telling the truth.
But my father's Slovakian was very poor.
He grew up as-- he grew up in a family where they spoke German.
And on the street, I guess, he picked up Hungarian, or, probably, his schooling was Hungarian.
No.
My mother came from a small town-- from a small village in Slovakia, so she spoke.
She didn't-- her Slovakian wasn't the literary Slovakian.
Her Slovakian was the-- was sort of Slovakian that was spoken in that region.
It wasn't the literary.
But she spoke that well.
She spoke well Hungarian.
And she also spoke German, and Yiddish, I guess.
Her parents-- in her house, they spoke Yiddish.

My grandmother came from Poland, just across the border-- well, at that time there were no borders between that part of Slovakia and Galicia, because all that belonged to the Hungary and Austrian Empire.
It's September of '44.
We-- through the grapevine, we found out that the Hungarians were upset that, I guess, the world opinion or-- or the Western countries, heard about the Auschwitz-- about Auschwitz.
And they were-- they threatened that the people will be brought to justice, who are doing-- perpetrators of the-- of that-- of these horrible things.
So the Hungarian government, or the Hungarian-- sort of the president of Hungary, or the-- he was regent, because Hungary was supposed to be a kingdom, and they didn't have a king.
So the head of the state was called the regent.
His name was Horthy.
And he decided-- he wanted, actually, to get out of the-- out of the war.
And the Germans were-- and so he formed a military government.
And that military government stopped all the deportations from the capital city of Budapest.
And-- and we-- we were told that the-- that there is a relaxation of anti-Semitism in Budapest, and people sort of are breathing more easily in Budapest.
The rest of the country of Hungary was already without Jews.
So we decided that maybe our chances would be better to go to Hungary than to stay in Slovakia.
We were making contacts with people who had-- who had to cross the border.
And our plans were definitely to leave Slovakia and go to Hungary.
So I-- it was Yom Kippur, after Yom Kippur, when we decided.
The day after Yom Kippur, it was in beginning of October, that we decided that we'll-- we'll go to make some contacts with someone to take us back to Hungary.
We took the bus from the little town.
We sort of felt more safe, safer too, even though we had to walk several miles to take a bus from next village back to Bratislava, instead of taking the train.
There was a train station in that village.
So we walked to the bus station, and we boarded the bus.
And our plan was to go to Bratislava to do some contacts through some Jewish friends and-- who had non-Jews-- Jewish contacts who would smuggle us over the border to Hungary.
Boarded the bus.
When the bus got to the outskirts of Bratislava, the bus was stopped, was boarded by a Hungarian and a German agent, an SS person.
And they announced-- they asked us to produce our identity cards.
At that point in time, we were-- everyone in Slovakia was to have had a Slovakian picture identity, ID.
Based on our birth certificate, we were able to go to the police station and secure a-- a-- an ID card, without pictures.
They gave us-- everyone-- it was such a big rush for everyone to get the ID cards, so they didn't really-- we just went in.
We showed our birth certificate.
We filled out some forms, and based on that they issued a-- an ID card.
So when the Slovakian agent that was a Hlinka guard-- it was the equivalent of an SS person in Germany, approached me.
I showed him my ID card.
He gave it back to me, found it in order.
It said that I was Roman Catholic.
And then the German SS person came, who must've been a Slovakian nationalist, but they were the German minority who lived in Slovakia.
He also gave it back to me, didn't find anything wrong with me, with my appearance, or with anything.
Then my father was sitting next to me, came to my father, the Slovakian took his ID card, and he gave it back to him.
When the German came to my father, he asked him in German when he was baptized.
[SPEAKING GERMAN], meaning when he was baptized.
He made-believe he doesn't understand German.
Had he understood him, he would have given himself right away-- away, because the Germans-- the Jews were the ones who spoke German, Slovakian, and Hungarian.
The Slovakians, if they were not German-- of German extraction, they didn't speak German.
They didn't even understand German.
So he called his Slovakian counterpart over.
I knew right away that that's-- that that's not good, because my father didn't speak Slovakian.
He just spoke a little bit, just like we speak Spanish here.
So he asked him in Slovakian when he was baptized.
So he said he wasn't baptized, that he's Roman Catholic.
They took him down-- the German fellow told him to take him down.
Well, at that point, I knew that-- that he'll-- he won't come back.
So I sort of-- when they grabbed him.
They called one of the Slovakian guards to take him.
I mean, he didn't put up any resistance.
There was no reason.
He still hoped, you know, who knows, you know?
Maybe-- maybe they'll just look at the paper.
Maybe they'll call the headquarters.
But they examined him.
And at that point, I wanted to get up and sort of give myself up, you know.
I felt, I'll go with him, you know-- whatever, whatever.
Was-- wasn't-- maybe it wouldn't have been a wise decision.
My father gave me a stern look, you know, like, you know, don't-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] don't move.
So I stayed there, you know.
I sort of felt, you know, maybe I should go back to my younger brother.
And I felt sort of-- and I still, up today, I feel bad.
Maybe I should've gone with him.
Maybe I would have been next to him.
It's true, he got sick.
He contacted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] typhus, and he-- he died in [NON-ENGLISH] I believe, in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in March of '45, so I was told.
I still feel bad, you know.
Maybe I could have-- had I been with him-- [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I would have helped him.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, that's, you know-- that was the end of-- when I got to Bratislava, I went to the Jewish area there.
And I saw there were no Jews there.
I right away realized that-- that that was the day when they rounded up all the Jews, and-- and they took them to-- to a city, Sered.
That was the-- that was the city where they rounded up all the Jews from Slovakia, and they took them all to Auschwitz.
I took the train back to [NON-ENGLISH], and I had no problem.
They checked my identity papers, and we came back.
I came back to my brother, who was very upset, you know.
Even though I went to work, I still felt, maybe I should go back, maybe.
But my friend-- and we decided, well, we wouldn't help them.
Maybe by staying out, we would-- we would be-- to go into their hands, we still had time, we felt.
So we stayed out there.
We still wanted to pursue the-- our plan to go back to Hungary, since the situation in Hungary was much better than here.
There was-- there were no Jews anymore.
The only Jews that were there were in hiding.
And we had no contact with those Jews.
Well, you know, we tried to-- to do whatever we can.
Even-- we had even money.
We had dollars.
But as long as the Jews were in Slovakia, we could change the dollars to Slovakian currency, but we couldn't do anything.
My father took some money with him, too.
I'm sure the Germans got them.
And so we decided-- we knew about-- we knew that in that village, there was one of the farmers was hiding an automobile, a new automobile that belonged to the Croatian ambassador to Slovakia.
So we clinged to any straw at that point.

So we thought, let's go and approach the-- that Hungarian farmer, and tell him that we would like to get in touch with the ambassador and tell him that we are from Eastern Slovakia-- not the Jews, you know, God forbid that we should give up our identity-- that our parents live in Eastern Slovakia, and there was no way of getting to Eastern Slovakian.
Whether he could take us through-- to Hungary.
And then from Hungary we'll go through Budapest.
We'll go back to Eastern Slovakia, because the Slovakian railroad was sabotaged by Russian and Slovakian partisans.
Well, it was something that would have been believable.
Several people were using the Hungarian railroads to go into Eastern Slovakia.
And we felt, once we are in Hungary, we'll go to Budapest.
He, in turn, told us that he doesn't think that he would jeopardize his automobile.
That's his baby.
The diplomat-- no, we didn't talk to the diplomat.
The farmer.
He's not using it.
He's afraid that they'll-- that they'll either, the Hungarians or the Germans, will take it away from him.
And he wanted to sort of survive.
I didn't know that my paternal grand-uncle knew the-- that-- the ambassador.
I had no idea.
He never told me.
In fact, when the round-up-- during the round-up, when they knocked on their door, that building belonged to my uncle.
It was a five-storey building, just underneath the castle of-- of Bratislava.
They had a plan.
They had two apartments-- one adjoining the other.
And they sort of had a hole in between the two apartments.
And the plan was that every night they'll padlock the other apartment.
And there was like an armoire where they kept their clothes, didn't have glass, that armoire, just, like, with a closet.
And they kept their clothes.
But the plan was, if there's a round-up, and the-- and the Slovakians are rounding up the Jews, they'll see the other apartment had a padlock.
So they'll think, well, no one outside a padlock-- you can't-- you know, it was a good excuse.
So they'll-- the family will go into hiding, into that apartment.
And one of them will give himself up.
He'll open the door, will tell them, I'm the only one here.
The rest of the family is away.
And so that's what happened.
My uncle was the one that stayed.
My grand-uncle was the one that stayed in the apartment.
The rest of the family- his wife, his daughter, his grandson, and his two sons, and daughter-in-law, and, I believe, three other children, went into the other apartment.
And they closed-- they sealed this apartment.
And my great-uncle was taken to camp, and, I guess, gassed.
And the rest of the family stayed a couple of days in that apartment.
Then they went out, and they stayed in hiding with the Croatian ambassador, in his residence.
Who was-- well, I don't know how he became the Croatian resident, but they must've been-- he must've paid him off.
And they were-- they were in hiding until after the war.
So technically, had I stayed with my grand-uncle, or my father, conceivably, unless they would have said, well, you go with your grand-uncle.
But we didn't know it.
So he-- that farmer told us, he says, there's no way you'll get him to-- to take you to Hungary, no matter how much money you would pay.
But we have a friend-- I have a friend who is a pharmaceutical student at the University of Bratislava.
He plans to go back to Eastern Slovakia, and we can go with him.
So we contacted him, and we still didn't tell him what we are.
We just told him that we our from a city by name of Secovce, and we would like to get back to Eastern Slovakia.
So his plan was, he says, you'll never get back to Slovakia, to Eastern Slovakia, but that he has some friends in a city by name-- in a little village near the city of Obisovce, in Eastern Slovakia.
The trains still run to that city-- although, sometimes you have to, where the bridges are-- are bombed, we could just walk over.
There is a train that takes you up to the end of that bridge, and then you cross on a pontoon bridge.
And then other train awaits you and you go-- that if he wants-- he wants to get back, he'll take us back, because we told him, well, we'll pay him a lot of money if he takes us.
So that was our plan.
So he said, OK.
He had a beautiful apartment in Bratislava.
So he told us since the train starts out early in the morning, we better come over, sleep over in his apartment.
Slept on the floors.
It was a beautiful place.
We hated to leave that place.
And we boarded the train.
And at that time, we were told that the train will go all the way through.
They repaired the-- that breach.
We took-- he took us-- we bought our tickets.
We all went separately, just in case one of us-- one of us is caught.
And we traveled.
Got our-- we went through security.
And also that pharmaceutical student.
And luckily, no one bothered us.
It was a full train, full of farmers.
And we sort of had a low profile, kept a low profile, and no one bothered us.
In fact, they offered us food.

And we arrived, I think, after a 12-hour journey, we arrived to that train station, Obisovce, where he arranged, with a-- on a wagon, we were taken to that remote village off the main road, and off the main railroads.

We came there to one of the farmers, and we told them that we-- we want-- we are-- we would like-- we want to enter hiding, because we don't want the Germans to take us along, whether as laborers or as fighters.
At that time, there was-- the Slovakian army revolted against the Germans, and they were crushed right away.
But the government was still pro-Nazi.
It was a good excuse, especially to simple farm people.

And they suggested that we go into hiding instead of staying, because the German army-- or the German SS people, sometimes they came into the village just to be sure that some Russian partisans are not hiding out there.
They came to raid the village, and they left the people alone, but just to be sure that partisans-- because there were partisans who were blowing up bridges and disrupting communications.
And this was a good place to hide.
And there was a time when they were there.
That we should go into hiding.
We-- And they were afraid that if we-- if they are caught with some people who don't belong to the village, then they'll be-- they might harm them, also.
So they took us to one of the hiding places.
It was a log house on a mountainside in the forest, deep in the forest.
We did not use a path, a road, just we used-- we just went on the slope.
Without shoes it was very hard to track the snow.
They taught us how to use our foot instead of going just straight, just sideways to go out.
We got into the-- to that hiding place.
It was like an A-frame built little place, built very comfortable.
They had beds made out of logs and straw on top of it.
They had-- they fashioned even tables that was-- that was hinged up during the day.
And only when they were eating, they hinged it down.
And they had an oven there.
And they had a saw.
They had pots and pans.
They had lots of food.
There were, I believe, five or six people.
They were all intelligent people.
One of them was the brother of that fellow, pharmacist.
And that's where we found out that his mother-- that friends of our mother was Jewish.
But she was baptized many, many years ago.
She was married to our friend's father, who was a-- a-- he was a Russian Orthodox Catholic.
And he always was telling us, he doesn't know why he's hiding-- you know.
And the rest of them, one was a dentist.
And I don't remember the others, what the others were.
But they were all intelligent people, fairly young.
They were in their 30's.
I guess, they were single.
But there wasn't too much room for three of us, for me, my brother, and that friend to stay there.
At first we just slept on straw in between in their path.
And they were sort of angry at our student friend, that he brought us up here.
It-- they told him-- to arrange with another farmer who built also a place like this, a much bigger one, like an A-frame.
And he had a stove there too.
But no one lived there.
He-- that farmer prepared that hiding place just in case the Germans were ordered to evacuate, so his family and his livestock, he could hide out there until the battlefront crosses.
So we were taken there.
We were very upset.
Now, again, we'll have to care for ourselves.
And we didn't have-- we didn't have-- we need-- we didn't have any blankets.
We just covered ourselves with our winter coats.
And we had to cut our own wood for-- to keep us warm.
And we didn't even know how to cook.
We didn't even have pots and pans or anything.
We went-- so, well, but that was our fate.
It was in-- in November and December.
We all lived in that-- once-- once a week we went down.
Once myself, one my friend-- not that student.
That student lived in the village.
He had no problem living in the village.
So we went on and bought bread, butter, and whatever we could from the farmers.
No, no, we didn't.
It wasn't a chalet.
It was very-- maybe four feet high.
It was just built like out of logs.
And they had leaves on top of the logs.
And it was snow-covered so it blended in into the scenery.
No, no.
That was, I guess-- but whenever we bought provisions for that, we paid.
We also-- we also bought blankets, because it was cold.
And only at night we could heat.
During the day we couldn't use our heat.
We would have given us away to the Germans, had they seen the smoke arising out of the hillside.
